127.reason: Error: should be Visited[delV]=1 instead of Visited[i]=1; i is out of bounds after the loop

reasons.buggy-deepseek-chat-1: 1.0 , reason:  Error: should be Visited[delV]=1 instead of Visited[i]=1; i is out of bounds after the loop
reasons.buggy-deepseek-chat-2: 1.0 , reason:  Error: Should be Visited[delV]=1 instead of Visited[i]=1. This incorrectly sets Visited[n] (out of bounds) or the last index instead of the deleted vertex.
reasons.buggy-deepseek-reasoner-1: 1.0 , reason:  [Error: should be Visited[delV]=1; using i after loop incorrectly sets Visited[n]=1 instead of marking the deleted vertex as visited.]
reasons.buggy-deepseek-reasoner-2: 1.0 , reason:  Should be Visited[delV]=1; using i after loop sets out-of-bounds index n.
reasons.buggy-qwen3.6-plus-1: 1.0 , reason:  [Error: 'i' equals 'n' after the loop, should mark 'delV' as visited instead of 'i']
reasons.buggy-qwen3.6-plus-2: 1.0 , reason:  [Error: After the loop, i equals n. It should mark the deleted vertex as visited, so it should be Visited[delV]=1;]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-1: 0.25 , reason:  [BUG: After the loop, i equals n, so Visited[i] is out-of-bounds (index n), and also fails to mark delV as visited. Should be Visited[delV]=1;]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-2: 0.5 , reason:  [This line has a critical bug: after the loop, 'i' equals 'n', so Visited[i] accesses out-of-bounds memory. It should be Visited[delV]=1, not Visited[i]=1. This causes undefined behavior and incorrect traversal after deletion.]

6.reason: [Using 'delete' as a variable name is invalid in C++ but allowed in C; however, it may cause issues with some compilers or tools that treat it as a keyword. More critically, 'V' and 'E' are declared but never used, while 'dots' and 'bian' are used inconsistently with problem description (should be n and m). However, the main error causing logical failure is elsewhere.]

75.reason: [This return 0 after printing "EMPTY!" causes undefined behavior when the queue is empty because BFS expects a valid vertex index. However, the problem states the graph is connected and deletion doesn't disconnect it, so this may not trigger. The real critical bug is below.]

139.reason: [Using 'delete' as a variable name is problematic in some environments, though technically valid in pure C. However, the compilation error message suggests a toolchain issue, but the actual logical error is in tBFS. The reported "gcc: not found" is likely a red herring from the test environment; the real bug is the out-of-bounds access in tBFS.]
